pages.text: Prediction of elastic modulus of normal and high strength concrete by support vector machine Kezhen Yan *, Caijun Shi College of Civil Engineering, Hunan University, Changsha, Hunan 410082, China article info Article history: Received 8 February 2009 Received in revised form 24 December 2009 Accepted 15 January 2010 Available online 13 February 2010 Keywords: Support vector machine Concrete Compressive strength Elastic modulus abstract Elastic modulus is an important property of concrete and is used to calculate deformation of structures. Support vector machine (SVM) is ﬁrmly based on learning theory and uses regression technique by intro- ducing accuracy insensitive loss function. This paper investigates the use of SVM to predict elastic mod- ulus of normal and high strength concrete. The elastic modulus predicted by SVM was compared with the experimental data and those from other prediction models. SVM demonstrated good performance and proven to be better than other models. /C2112010 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved. 1. Introduction Elastic modulus is an important property of concrete materials and is extensively used to calculate deformation of structures [1].I t can be obtained from the stress–strain responses of concrete under compression. However, measuring elastic modulus of concrete is a relatively elaborate testing involving cyclic loading and strain mea- surements. It is complicated and time-consuming compared with measuring compressive strength. Therefore, many researchers and engineers have tried to predict the elastic modulus using the- oretical and empirical approaches based on compressive strength of concrete, which can avoid the difﬁculties encountered during the measurement of elastic modulus. Elastic modulus can usually be estimated from compressive strength. Pauw recommended an empirical formula Ec ¼ A ﬃﬃﬃ ﬃ f 0 p by regression on a large set of data [2]. ACI building code [3] and TS 500 [4] used the following two equations between elastic modulus Ec (in GPa) and compressive strength fc (in MPa) for normal strength concrete (NSC): Ec ¼ 4:73 ðfc Þ1=2 ð1Þ Ec ¼ 3:25 ðfc Þ1=2 þ 14 ð2Þ Relationships between elastic modulus and compressive strength for high strength concrete (HSC) are different from that for normal strength concrete. For example American [5], European [6], and Norwegian [7] committees recommended the following equations for HSC, respectively: Ec ¼ 3:32 ðfc Þ1=2 þ 6:9 ð3Þ Ec ¼ 10 ðfc þ 8Þ1=3 ð4Þ Ec ¼ 9:5 ðfc Þ0:3 ð5Þ where fc is compressive strength (in MPa) and Ec is elastic modulus (in GPa). Mathematical regression models are usually imperfect descrip- tion of complex physical phenomena. The accuracy of the esti- mated results depends on the size of available data. Artiﬁcial neural network (ANN) and the fuzzy logic model have been used for prediction of elastic modulus for NSC and HSC [8,9]. However, there are some shortcomings for ANN, such as slow convergence speed, poor generalizing performance, arriving at local minimum and over-ﬁtting problems. Furthermore, there is no proper method to determine the number of hidden layers. Fuzzy logic model determines the fuzzy rules with difﬁculty. The support vector ma- chine (SVM) is a new, efﬁcient and novel approach to improve gen- eralization performance and can attain a global minimum. SVMs achieve good generalization ability by adopting a structural risk minimization induction principle that aims at minimizing a bound on generalization error of a model rathe

capacities of the SVMs. The root mean square error (RMSE) values were compared to research at a suitable choice of kernels. Two types of kernel functions have proved to be better than other kernels, namely radial basis function and polynomial function, for NSC and HSC, respectively. The input and output parameters are compressive strength and elastic modulus of concrete. The parameters c and e of ee-insensitive loss function greatly affect the results of SVM model, the values of c, e and other kernel-spe- ciﬁc parameters were chosen by a trial and error approach. In the training process, the root mean squares error (RMSE) and the mean absolute percentage error (MAPE) were used as the main cri- teria to evaluate the performance of the SVM model. Different combinations of c and e values were examined to get the best per- formance with respect to the training data. The values of c, e and c of RBF are 100, 11 /C2/C2 1011 /C2 10-6/C0 6 and 0.058 for NSC prediction model, and c, e and d of polynomial are 1000, 0.001and 0.001 and 6 for HSC prediction model with the best performance, respec- tively. The errors (predicted values subtract measured values) computed by SVM model, other models and measured values of elastic modulus are shown in Tables 1 and 2 for HSC and in Tables 3 and 4 for NSC. For convenient comparison purpose, the experi- mental and predicted results are plotted in Figs. 1–4. It can be seen that the results from SVM method is in good agreement with the experimental results. Elastic modulus increases with the increase of compressive strength of concrete, but the relationship between elastic modulus and compressive strength cannot be precisely expressed by a for- mula. Comparison of experimental results with SVM for testing of HSC for testing of NSC. K. Yan, C. Shi / Construction and Building Materials 24 (2010) 1479–1485 1483

percentage error (MAPE) were calculated for all these methods and are plotted in Figs. 5 and 6 for HSC and Figs. 7 and 8 for NSC. The model with the smallest RSME and MAPE can be regarded as the best model. From Figs. 5 and 7, it can be seen that MAPE of the SVM is smaller than other models, the values are 3.13% and 8.42% for HSC and NSC in training, respectively, while are 3.75% and 9.69% for HSC and NSC during testing. It can be seen from Figs. 6 and 8 that RMSE of the SVM is smaller than other methods for HSC. The SVM model result for NSC is smaller than ACI 318 and TS 500 and is very close to other models. The analysis shows that the SVM can predict the elastic modulus of HSC and NSC well. Although ANN model demonstrated good performance to predict elastic modulus too, it contains a large number of controlling parameters, including the number of hidden layers, the number of hidden nodes, learning rate, momentum term, the number of training epochs, transfer functions and weight initialization meth- ods. Obtaining an optimal combination of these parameters is much more difﬁcult. The SVM model contains only three parame- ters (c, e and the one kernel parameters), and uses the SRM princi- ple in deﬁning cost function provided more generalization capacity than ANN model. Another advantage of SVM model is its optimiza- tion algorithm, which includes solving a linearly constrained qua- dratic programming function and leads to a unique, optimal and global solution compared to ANN model [18]. Further more, SVM model has better generalization capability to avoid overtraining than ANN model. Thus, SVM model can be regarded as a very effec- tive method to predict elastic modulus of all grades of concrete from their compressive strength. 4. Conclusion This study has investigated the use of SVM for prediction of elastic modulus of concrete from compressive strength. RMSE and MAPE of the predicted results by SVM method were the small- est for HSC among all the prediction methods. They were very close to those by ANN model but smaller than those from AC 318 and TS 500 codes for NSC. However, SVM has better generalization capa- bility than ANN model. Thus, SVM can be regarded as a very effec- tive method for prediction of elastic modulus of all grades of concrete from their compressive strength. References [1] Mesbah HA, Lacherni M, Aitcin PC. (RMSE) and the mean absolute percentage error (MAPE) were used as criteria to compare the performance of SVM model and other models. 2. Support vector machine SVM is based on statistical learning theory developed by Vapnik [10]. It is effective to improve generalization performance and can obtain globally optimal and unique solution. It is generally used for pattern recognition and regression estimation. Recently, with the introduction of Vapnik’s e-insensitive loss function, SVM has been extended to solve non-linear regress estimations and time series [11]. In SVM regression, the basic objective is to minimize an upper bound of the generalization error based on the structural risk min- imization (SRM). The detailed description of SVM can be found in the publications by Vapnik [10,11]. The main idea of SVM regression is to map the input data x into a high-dimensional feature space by a non-linear mapping and to do linear regression in this space. The regression model is deﬁned as y = f(x)+ e, where x and y are input and output function, respec- tively, and deﬁned in the high-dimensional feature space; e is the independently random error [12]. Given a dataset G ¼f ðx i; yiÞ; i ¼ 1; 2 ...lg ; where l is the size of the training data set, xi is the value of the input and yi is the value of output. The regression SVM seeks to ﬁnd a function that gives a deviation of e from the actual output. The optimum regression function can be assumed as follows: f ðxÞ¼ x /C1 /ðxÞþ b ð6Þ where x is the weight vector, b is the scalar threshold, and u(x) rep- resents the high-dimensional feature spaces which are nonlinearly mapped from the input space x. The coefﬁcients x and b are esti- mated by minimizing the regularized risk function: Rreg ðCÞ¼ C 1 l Xl i¼1 Leðyi; f ðxiÞÞ þ 1 2 kxk2 ð7Þ Leðy; f ðxÞÞ ¼ jy /C0 f ðxÞj /C0 e for jy /C0 f ðxÞj P e 0 otherwise /C26 ð8Þ where the ﬁrst term C 1 l Pl i¼1Leðyi; f ðxiÞÞ is the empirical error, and the second term 1 2 kxk2 measures the ﬂatness of the function, Le (y, f(x)) is the e-insensitive loss function. The parameter C deter- mines the trade-off between the empirical error and the ﬂatness of the model. Introducing the slack variables ni and n/C3 i into the Eq. (7), it can be transformed to the dual optimization problem: Minimize : Rðx; b; n/C3 ; nÞ¼ 1 2 kxk2 þ C Pl i¼1 ðni þ n/C3 i Þ ð9Þ yi /C0 x /C1 /ðxiÞ/C0 b 6 e þ ni Subjected to x /C1 /ðxiÞþ b 6 e þ n/C3 i n/C3 i ; n 6 0 i ¼ 1; 2; ...; l ð10Þ where the parameters ni and n/C3 i are slack variables that determine the degree to which samples with error more than e be penalized. In other words, any error less than e does not require ni and n/C3 i , and hence does not enter the objective function because these data points have a value of zero for the loss function. The optimization problem (Eqs. (9) and (10)) can be solved by Lagrangian multipliers, and its solution is given by: f ðx; ai; a/C3 i Þ¼ Xnsv i¼1 ðai /C0 a/C3 i Þð/ðxiÞ/C1ð xjÞÞ þ b ð11Þ where ( ai) and ( a/C3 i ) are the Lagrangian multipliers, and nsv is the number of support vectors. An important aspect is that only some of Lagrangian multipliers ( ai; a/C3 i ) are nonzero values, and the train- ing data points have approximate errors equal to or larger than e. The training data with nonzero Lagrangian multipliers are called support vectors. Generally, the larger the e is, the fewer the number of support vectors is. However, a larger e can also depreciate the approximation accuracy placed on the tr

SVMs is largely characterized by the type of its kernel function, it is important to choose the appropriate kernel function and kernel parameters for each application problem in order to guarantee sat- isfactory results. The main drawback of the SVMs is determination of the kernel parameters, as this is still a heuristic process. In this paper, the kernel-speciﬁc parameters will be chosen by a trail– and-error approach. 3. SVM for prediction of elastic modulus of NSC and HSC As mentioned above, many methods have been proposed for the prediction of elastic modulus of concrete from its compressive strength. This study attempts to use SVM for the prediction of elas- tic modulus. The experimental results from Wee [14] and Gesoglu [15] for HSC, Ozturan [16] and Turan [17] for NSC, are used in this study. It uses 69 out of 89 cases for HSC and 57 out of 70 cases for NSC for training data set, the rest are considered as testing data set, as previously used for ANN [8]. The data are normalized before being used in the model as following: x ¼½ xi /C0ð xiÞmin/C138 =½ðxiÞmax /C0ð xiÞmin/C138ð 13Þ where (xi)max and (xi)min are the maximum and minimum values of input and output data, respectively. A number of kernels are discussed in the literature. But it is important to choose one, which gives the best generalization with a given data set.
